The Dutch fire broke out in the Klamath National Forest at 1620 PST on the afternoon of 07/31/10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dutch Fire started at 1620 PDT on 07/31/10, and the cause is under investigation.  It was contained at 1800 PDT on 08/04/10 at 371 acres.  2.2 million dollars was expended on fire fighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms and frequent lightning ignited fires in Joshua Tree NP.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chief Ranger at JTNP reported lightning started a brush fire that burned for two days and consumed about 110 acres in the Keys View area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low passed through northern California and northern Nevada from the 18th to the 19th bringing gusty winds to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South-southwest to southwest winds on Lake Tahoe were sustained between 20 to 25 mph from late morning to early evening on the 18th. The winds (and waves it generated) were sufficient to sink 3 boats.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sudden blast of wind, estimated up to 45 mph, struck 6 miles north of Bard. Very low visibility was observed, estimated to be about a quarter mile due to blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass brought freezing temperatures to areas east of the Cascades in South Central Oregon and Northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 29 to 33 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Vernon Wildfire occurred in Yosemite National Park. The ignition date was August 8, 2010, at 0000 PDT. The cause was lightning. The location was 21 Miles North-Northwest of Yosemite Valley in Tuolumne County. The size was 909 acres. The fire was contained on October 3. No fatalities or damages occurred. The cost was $50,000.

The Sheep Wildfire occurred in Sequoia/Kings Canyon National Park. The ignition date was July 16, 2010, at 1200 PDT, and the cause was lightning. The location was 1.5 miles southwest of Cedar Grove in Fresno County along the Tulare County border. The size was 9007 acres (3119 acres in Kings Canyon NP, 5888 acres in Sequoia Forest). The wildfire was extinguished by rain between October 3-6. There were no fatalities or lost structures, and the cost was $1,567,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm originating in the Gulf of Alaska on the 9th dropped south to off the California coast on the 11th before moving into California and Nevada on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July began with a weak upper-level trough of low pressure over California. As a result, the first day of the month saw temperatures across the central and southern San Joaquin Valley a couple of degrees below normal, with slightly cooler temperatures the next day. The trough began to lift northeast out of California on July 3rd, but a northwest flow aloft down the back side of the trough brought gusty winds to the Tehachapi Mountains and the Kern County desert area around Mojave. Winds in those areas gusted to 45 to 55 mph during the early morning of the 3rd. As the trough moved out of central California, an upper-level ridge began building into the state. This allowed temperatures to warm a couple of degrees, although central and southern San Joaquin Valley highs continued slightly below normal.

The second in a series of powerful winter storms wreaked havoc on Southern California on January 19th. A strong southerly 925 mb jet ahead of the cold front contributed to moderate low-level wind shear. A line of thunderstorms with embedded rotation moved through Orange County late in the morning, bringing waterspouts, at least one tornado, and hurricane force winds. Strong thunderstorm winds also impacted San Diego County, contributing to structural damage and one fatality. Large, steep surf was also observed with this storm in both Orange and San Diego counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a line of thunderstorms moved through Orange County, a peak wind gust of 93 MPH was measured by the weather station at the Newport Beach Pier. Several wind gusts in excess of 70 MPH were also reported. Several boats in the Newport Beach Harbor broke free. In Costa Mesa, just inland from Newport Beach, a building in an industrial park on 17th Ave sustained moderate damage. Approximately two-thirds of the top layer of the roof blew off. Pieces of the roof, some as large as 15 feet long, trees, and other debris littered the parking lot. Across the street at a mobile home park, metal awnings were ripped off by the wind and pieces of wood were seen flying through the air. A few blocks away near the intersection of Harbor Blvd and Victoria Street, the strong winds blew open the door to a business and shattered a window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season storm dropped down across Central and Southern California, bringing heavy rain and flash flooding. The storm produced two impulses of rainfall between the 19th and 21st. The heaviest rainfall occurred across San Luis Obispo, Santa Barbara and Ventura Counties with rainfall totals ranging from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across coastal areas to between 1.50 and 4.00 inches in the foothills and mountains. Across Los Angeles Counties, the rainfall totals were less, ranging between 0.25 and 0.75 inches across coastal areas to between 1.00 and 3.00 inches in the foothills and mountains. The heavy rain produced some flash flooding across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara Counties. Snow levels did not drop until after a majority of the rainfall had fallen, so only 2 to 4 inches of snowfall was reported above 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in the community of Santa Maria. A trained weather spotter reported one half inch of rain in 5 minutes. With such intense rainfall, there were numerous reports of significant flooding in the city of Santa Maria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of powerful winter storms wreaked havoc on Southern California on January 19th. A strong southerly 925 mb jet ahead of the cold front contributed to moderate low-level wind shear. A line of thunderstorms with embedded rotation moved through Orange County late in the morning, bringing waterspouts, at least one tornado, and hurricane force winds. Strong thunderstorm winds also impacted San Diego County, contributing to structural damage and one fatality. Large, steep surf was also observed with this storm in both Orange and San Diego counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a line of thunderstorms moved through south Orange County between 1330 and 1400 PST on the 19th. A spotter reported wind damage at his mother's condominium complex in Laguna Niguel, near Oso Parkway and Highway 73. A twelve foot tall tennis court enclosure with 4 inch aluminum posts was bent outwards at a 25 degree angle, two mature trees with a trunk diameter of greater than 12 inches were snapped off just above the ground level, and numerous 4 inch diameter tree branches from a pine tree broke off. The spotter was not at the location at the time, but observed the damage two months after it happened. The mother and another neighbor felt a suction and the windows of their condos shook violently. It is possible that a tornado briefly touched down in the complex, though no other reports of a tornado could be found.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 20 mudslides, due to rain and pre-loosened dirt, poured over Varner Road in Cathedral City in Riverside County on the morning of October 19. Thirty-eight hundredths of an inch of rain fell in the desert that day, resulting in the closure of Varner Road between Date Palm and Rio Del Sol due to an area of mud up to 60 feet wide and up to 18 inches deep. Two vehicles were trapped in the mud, which was cleared by 1000 PST October 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tsunami generated by an 8.8 magnitude earthquake off the coast of Chile reached the Southern California coast shortly after noon on February 27. The tsunami caused tidal surges of four to five feet over the course of about two hours. The speed of the currents in both Orange and San Diego counties were estimated to be 8 to 12 knots. The tsunami caused minor damage to structures and vessels, mainly in harbors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bait barge at Dana Point Harbor was snapped in half by a series of tsunami surges. A 12 knot current was observed with a three inch wave in the main channel. The depth in the main channel fell to 6 feet as the water receded. The normal depth in the channel is 12 to 15 feet.

The first storm made its way through the region on January 18th. Heavy rain, mountain snow, and strong winds contributed to widespread damage and urban flooding, as well as a few flash floods with minor debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain-soaked soil combined with gusty winds caused a large Eucalyptus tree to fall down on North Torrey Pines Road, damaging two vehicles. The occupants of the vehicles were not injured.

The tsunami caused minor damage to structures and vessels, mainly in harbors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of tsunami surges destroyed a section of dock on Shelter Island and damaged an underwater pipe. Several vessels in America's Cup Harbor also came loose from their moorings. Some concrete pilings were snapped after a couple commercial fishing boats that were tied to them were pulled away by the strong currents. At Oceanside Harbor, all but one of the channel buoys were lifted and dragged by the surges. The buoys later washed up on the beaches. Currents associated with the surges were estimated at 8 to 10 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of powerful winter storms wreaked havoc on Southern California on January 19th. A strong southerly 925 mb jet ahead of the cold front contributed to moderate low-level wind shear. A line of thunderstorms with embedded rotation moved through Orange County late in the morning, bringing waterspouts, at least one tornado, and hurricane force winds. Strong thunderstorm winds also impacted San Diego County, contributing to structural damage and one fatality. Large, steep surf was also observed with this storm in both Orange and San Diego counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding in areas with poor drainage in Fontana and Redlands. In Fontana, a home was filled with several inches of water after sandbags surrounding the home failed. In Redlands, a clogged drain caused an apartment parking lot to flood, damaging several cars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were measured by the Julian mesonet between 1425 and 1325 PST. Wind gusts to 61 MPH were measured during that time. Patio furniture in Julian was picked up by the wind and ended up in a neighbors tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure subsequently built along the California coast beginning November 14th, bringing a warming trend that saw high temperatures as much as 10 degrees above normal. In addition to the warming trend, the northerly flow around the periphery of the ridge brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts. The warming trend lasted through the 18th, then the ridge gave way to a deep upper-level trough. This storm system brought a push of unseasonably cold air to the central California interior. Fresno had a high temperature of 74 on November 18th; the next day,|the high was only 60. Bakersfield had a 20-degree change in high temperatures, falling from a high of 79 on November 18th to a high of only 59 degrees on the 19th.|The storm brought the heaviest rainfall for the month to interior central California. The 5-day storm total rainfall for Fresno was 1.23 inch, which accounted for 68.3 percent of the total of 1.80 inch for the month. This 5-day total also was 111.8 % of the normal rainfall for the entire month (1.10 inch). For the same period, Bakersfield recorded 0.54 inch of rain, 64.3 percent of its monthly total of 0.84 inch. The 5-day rainfall nearly matched the normal for November at Bakersfield of 0.59 inch.||In the Southern Sierra Nevada, a total of up to 3 feet of snow fell in some locations above 6000 feet, while in the Kern County Mountains, 4 inches of new snow fell down to 5000 feet. The snow level plunged into the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills, with 7 inches of snow falling at Ponderosa Basin.||Very strong winds occurred in the Kern County Deserts on the afternoon of the 23rd. The highest winds were measured at Blue Max near Walker Pass and Bear Peak in the Southern Sierra. Gusts in those areas topped out at 80-90 mph with a peak gust of 93 mph at 632 pm at Blue Max.

An upper low near the California coast created conditions for isolated strong thunderstorms north and northwest of Truckee on the afternoon of the 9th.

A cold trough digging down the California coast brough several waves of precipitation to the area. Several inches of snow fell in the San Bernardino mountains as well as some strong, gusty winds in the mountains and deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Apple and Lucerne Valley area around 0150 PST and continued through 1600 PST. Between 1451 and 1551 PST, a peak wind gust of 78 MPH was measured by the Burns Canyon RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to the higher elevations of the northern and central Sierra Nevada Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of snow and gusty southwest wind resulted in blowing snow and poor visibilities above 7500 feet, including on the higher trans-Sierra Nevada passes and in some of the higher campgrounds of the area.  Several feet of snow fell at the highest elevations in the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another in a series of winter storms moved through on this date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No heavy snow reports from this storm as this area is sparsely populated.  However, based on heavy precipitation recorded in adjacent zone CAZ082, including 3.55 inches at the CALTRANS site at Snowman's Summit, it is assumed that heavy snow fell in this zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season storm dropped down across Central and Southern California, bringing heavy rain and flash flooding. The storm produced two impulses of rainfall between the 19th and 21st. The heaviest rainfall occurred across San Luis Obispo, Santa Barbara and Ventura Counties with rainfall totals ranging from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across coastal areas to between 1.50 and 4.00 inches in the foothills and mountains. Across Los Angeles Counties, the rainfall totals were less, ranging between 0.25 and 0.75 inches across coastal areas to between 1.00 and 3.00 inches in the foothills and mountains. The heavy rain produced some flash flooding across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara Counties. Snow levels did not drop until after a majority of the rainfall had fallen, so only 2 to 4 inches of snowfall was reported above 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding in southern San Luis Obispo County. Due to heavy rain, the roadway was completely washed away at the intersection of Highway 1 and Division Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong offshore flow, set up by strong high pressure in the Great Basin and a thermal Low off the California coast, caused strong winds in the Inland Empire, downing tree limbs and knocking out power to thousands of customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Santa Ana mountain foothills between 0530 and 0630 PST and continued to between 0930 and 1030 PST. Between 0820 and 0920 PST, multiple gusts above 58 MPH occurred. A peak wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by the Fremont Canyon RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms with short lived breaks between them struck northern California.  Initially the snow level was above most populated areas...but it lowered to many of those locations late in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Shasta Avalanche Center reported 7 inches of snow in 12 hours at 5870 feet 10SW of Mt. Shasta City ending at 18/0700 PST. A spotter ESE Mount Shasta City reported 8 inches of snow in 20 hours ending at 20/1300 PST, then another 2 inches in the following 12 hours.  A spotter SSE Mount Shasta City reported 7.5 inches of snow in about 12 hours ending at 20/0845 PST.  A spotter 4SW Weed reported 8 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 20/0700 PST. A spotter 2SW Mount Shasta City reported 8 inches of snow in around 12-15 hours ending at 20/1145 PST.  The cooperative observer in Mt. Shasta City reported 11.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 20/2300 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another in a series of winter storms moved through on this date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No heavy snow reports from this storm as most of the snow apparently fell above populated areas.  However, heavy rain was reported at lower elevations and sufficiently low temperatures were recorded at higher elevations.  Therefore, it is assumed that heavy snow fell in this zone. Reported 24 hour rainfall amounts included 2.65 inches at the Somes Bar RAWS, 1.96 inches at the Sawyer's Bar RAWS, 1.90 inches at the Dutch-Indy RAWS, and 1.32 inches at the Scott Mtn near Callahan RAWS.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant storm of the season brought strong winds and heavy rain to the San Francisco Bay Area. A low pressure trough from the Gulf of Alaska dropped far enough south to drench the region causing trees to fall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind combined with heavy rain to topple trees and power lines causing road closures and power outages. In San Francisco's North Bay Area nearly 10,000 customers lost power due to trees falling onto power lines. In one instance, a tree knocked down a power line when it fell in Inverness Park near the intersection of Portola Avenue and Vallejo Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant storm of the season brought strong winds and heavy rain to the San Francisco Bay Area. A low pressure trough from the Gulf of Alaska dropped far enough south to drench the region causing trees to fall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flooding on State Highway 29 under Trancas Street. Three vehicles crashed after losing control in the flooded underpass. Two of the vehicles collided while the other ran into a guard rail. No one was hurt.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early storm for the season caused ocean waves to rise above ten feet leading to sets of higher waves washing onto area beaches. Also, stronger rip currents were occurring because of the enhanced wave action.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women were swept into the ocean by a sneaker wave at Carmel River Beach and taken out to sea by a rip current around 3:15 p.m. PST. One woman perished in the ocean and the other was rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough digging down the California coast brough several waves of precipitation to the area. Several inches of snow fell in the San Bernardino mountains as well as some strong, gusty winds in the mountains and deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Coachella Valley between 0050 and 0150 PST and continued through 0350 PST. Between 0150 and 0350 PST, peak wind gusts of 60 and 61 MPH were measured by the Whitewater RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Great Basin combined with northeast winds aloft produced a brief period of gusty Santa Ana winds on the morning of March 19th, mainly in the mountains and the Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Riverside County mountains between 0720 and 0820 PST and continued through 0920 PST. Between 0820 and 0920 PST, a peak wind gust of 65 MPH was measured by the Indian Mountain RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant storm of the season brought strong winds and heavy rain to the San Francisco Bay Area. A low pressure trough from the Gulf of Alaska dropped far enough south to drench the region causing trees to fall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a small debris slide to occur along State Highway 1 just north of Jenner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant storm of the season brought strong winds and heavy rain to the San Francisco Bay Area. A low pressure trough from the Gulf of Alaska dropped far enough south to drench the region causing trees to fall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to minor flooding of westbound Interstate 580 west of State Highway 24. An inch of water spread into about five feet of the right-hand lane. Also, The High Street on-ramp to northbound Interstate 880 was flooded due to poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought strong winds to the valleys of Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weed Airport RAWS measured south winds of 44 mph with a peak gust of 59 mph at 11/1048 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused three brush fires near Inspiration Point, Keys View and Upper Boy Scout Trailhead within the Joshua Tree National Park at approximately 1200 PST on August 26th. The fire grew to 110 acres and burned on rocky slopes with scattered pinon pines, junipers, Joshua trees and other desert brush. The road to Keys View from the Park Boulevard junction was temporarily closed to the public.
Severe storms moved northward throughout the Imperial Valley with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large eucalyptus trees blown down and an empty water tank rolled 100 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved northward throughout the Imperial Valley with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter in the town of Brawley reported golf-ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass combined with longer nights to bring sub-freezing temperatures to portions of South Central Oregon and Northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 30 to 36 degrees.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass combined with longer nights to bring sub-freezing temperatures to portions of South Central Oregon and Northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 28 to 37 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass combined with longer nights to bring sub-freezing temperatures to portions of South Central Oregon and Northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 30 to 35 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The heaviest rainfall and strongest winds of the week hit the deserts on Thursday...with brief torrential rains that left streets flooded and water in the streams and rivers. At Imperial over two inches of rain fell between the 18th and the 22nd, with 1.10 inches on the 21st. Winds resulted in damage to many areas...and record low barometric pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds associated with a line of storms caused widespread power outages. Power poles were downed and trees uprooted. At least two trucks were blown over on Interstate 10 near Blythe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Pacific Northwest with strong upslope flow across Northern California which resulted in heavy snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4SW Weed reported 8 inches of snow from 24/1500 PST to 25/0600 PST. Castle Lake weather station at 5900 feet 5W Dunsmuir reported 20 inches of snow from 04/0800 PST to 05/0800 PST. A report from the Snowman Summit station at elevation 4478 feet measured 2.62 inches of liquid precipitation through the entire warning period with 2.97 inches measured between 04/1040 PST and 05/0400 PST. Using a 6 to 1 snow ratio for this wet type snow event, this translates to 18 inches of snow in less than 18 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific plume of moisture ahead of an advancing trough of low pressure brought heavy rain and periods of serious flooding for nearly a week. The plume of moisture responsible was a form of an Atmospheric River. The mountain areas, particularly the San Bernardino range, experienced record large rainfall totals and many areas of flash flooding, debris flows and mud slides. Most rivers in the county warning area reached flood stage. Other effects include numerous traffic collisions, roadway flooding and closures, swift water rescues, beach closures and millions of dollars in damage to homes, businesses and infrastructure. Along with numerous city and governor-declared States of Emergency, President Obama proclaimed a Federal disaster declaration for 10 counties in California, including all four counties comprising the SGX CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Diego River at Fashion Valley has been above its flood stage of 11.3 feet since 1330 PST. A peak river stage of 14.01 feet was reported, making this a moderate flood event. The river flooding resulted in numerous closed roadways and portions of the Fashion Valley Mall parking facilities were under water. This river flooding also resulted in extensive flooding of the Qualcomm Stadium on Friars Road, including both the stadium itself and the surrounding parking lot. A total of about 1.5 million gallons of water had to be pumped out of that area, back into the river.|A flood channel overflowed, resulting in evacuations of 12 houses in Barrio Logan neighborhood in San Diego. Five people were evacuated from homes in Encanto due to stream flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed by flooding in Yucca Valley and Joshua Tree. One vehicle was stuck in the water on Mojave Ranch Road in Joshua Tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris were on several roads in the Joshua Tree area.  An elderly care center was also flooded.

Sewer pumps were also damaged, resulting in the closure of about 12 miles of Orange County beaches, due to the pollution and waste entered into the storm runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm from the Gulf of Alaska produced strong winds and heavy rain over portions of the San Francisco Bay Area and the Central California Coast. Some of the heavy rain was caused by thunderstorms, which dropped small hail as well. Rainfall amounts from this storm were as high as four inches over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy rain to cause power lines and trees to fall. The intersection of Graham Hill Road at Tanner Heights near Santa Cruz was closed after a live power line landed in the road. In all, Santa Cruz County experienced power outages to about 1100 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold low pressure dropped south along the Pacific Northwest coast and into the northern Sierra between the 18th and the 21st followed by another low on the 23rd. This brought gusty winds and periods of heavy snow to northeast California and the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow with this storm came in two waves, one from the evening of the 19th to the morning of the 20th and the second about 24 hours later. Widespread storm total snowfall of 2 to 3 feet were reported around Lake Tahoe, with local amounts to 4 feet near the crest.  In addition to heavy snow, winds gusted to between 35 and 45 mph especially on the 18th and 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm from the Gulf of Alaska produced strong winds and heavy rain over portions of the San Francisco Bay Area and the Central California Coast. Some of the heavy rain was caused by thunderstorms, which dropped small hail as well. Rainfall amounts from this storm were as high as four inches over the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with heavy rain dropped trees onto buildings, pushed cars off roads and caused power outages across the area. Power lines were knocked to the ground around 6:23 p.m. PST in the area of Empire Grade and Ice Cream Grade causing 800 customers to lose power. A total of 1100 customers lost power in Santa Cruz County. In another spot along Empire Grade downed power lines and trees blocked the entire road north of Smith Grade. Power lines also fell into trees along Lompico Road around 7 p.m. PST sparking a small fire. Power lines even fell onto a car on State Route 9 at Henry Cowell State Park around 7 p.m. PST.  In Felton around 7:15 p.m. PST a tree fell through the roof of a home on Buckeye Drive. The greatest damage occurred around 7:20 pm. PST when large trees fell into buildings in Mount Herman. Sequoia Lodge, an eight room facility, and a three-room cabin were significantly damaged when five foot diameter trees were uprooted by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of winter storm moved quickly through interior Northern California.  It brought a moderate amount of snow and rain to the area, but came on the heels of a much more significant winter storm. A large Pacific plume of moisture ahead of an advancing trough of low pressure brought heavy rain and periods of serious flooding for nearly a week. The plume of moisture responsible was a form of an Atmospheric River. The mountain areas, particularly the San Bernardino range, experienced record large rainfall totals and many areas of flash flooding, debris flows and mud slides. Most rivers in the county warning area reached flood stage. Other effects include numerous traffic collisions, roadway flooding and closures, swift water rescues, beach closures and millions of dollars in damage to homes, businesses and infrastructure. Along with numerous city and governor-declared States of Emergency, President Obama proclaimed a Federal disaster declaration for 10 counties in California, including all four counties comprising the SGX CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orange County officials estimate the total damages to public and private property at $36 million, due the 10+ of rain received by many areas. The city of Laguna Beach was certainly the hardest hit, making up approximately $12 million of the county's damages. Twelve miles of beaches in the county also had to be closed due to massive amounts of debris and pollution in storm runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued unsettled pattern remained over the CWA in association with a very active upper level jet and El Nino pattern. The California portion of the Medford CWA was  on the northern fringe of the area of heavy precipitation.  Significant snowfall accumulations were reported for northern California as upslope flow prevailed across the area.  This snow was very wet...about a 6 to 1 snow ration...and this caused many broken trees and branches to fall into power lines...resulting in widespread power outages.   Overall the 1/19-21/10 snowfall in Mt. Shasta City was 39 inches...the greatest 3 day total since 1970.   Widespread power outages began on 1/19/10 (Tuesday). Nearly 9000 Pacific Power customers were without power at the peak of the storm.  By 1/23/10 (Saturday), 1260 customers were still without power.  Virtually all customers had power back by 1/25/10 (Monday).  Siskiyou county, along with other counties in the state, was declared a disaster area due to this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter ESE Mount Shasta City reported 10 inches of snow in 9.5 hours between 20/0100 and 20/1030 PST, then 10-12 inches in 12 hours ending at 21/0030 PST.  Later he reported a total accumulation of 48 inches of snow in 41.5 hours between 19/1700 and 21/1030 PST.   A spotter 4SW Weed reported 24 inches of snow between 19/1600 and 20/1100 PST.  A spotter ESE Mount Shasta City reported 24 inches of snow in 48 hours between 18/2000 and 20/2000 PST. A spotter 4SSW Weed reported 18 inches of snow in 10.5 hours between 20/1030 and 20/2100 PST. A spotter 3NW Mount Shasta City reported 33 inches of snow in 48 hours between 18/2100 and 20/2100 PST. A spotter 6NW Mount Shasta City reported 7 inches of snow in 12 hours between 21/1900 and 22/0700 PST.  A spotter 4SW Weed reported 22 inches of snow in 16 hours between 21/1800 and 22/1000 PST.  A spotter 2SW Mount Shasta City reported 16-18 inches of snow in 24 hours between 20/1030 and 21/1030 PST with 24 inches of snow on the ground. A spotter 1NE Mount Shasta City reported 18 inches of new snow overnight (1/20-21/10) and 48 inches total.  Power was out and numerous trees were down.  Power in some areas was out 4-5 days and avalanche warnings were issued for Mount Shasta.  The cooperative observer in Mount Shasta City reported 17 inches of snow in 21 hours between 19/2300 and 20/2000 PST, then 10 more inches of snow in 24 hours between 20/2000 and 21/2000 PST.  The cooperative observer in McCloud reported 10.5 inches of snow in 24 hours between 20/0800 and 21/0800 PST.

Areas that were still digging out and drying out from the previous storm were delayed in their recovery as travelers headed toward their holiday destinations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell at the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved through interior Northern California the weekend prior to Thanksgiving.  The storm was cold enough to result in snow falling in the foothill regions of the Coastal, Northern, and Sierra Nevada Mountain ranges while also wet enough to produce more than six feet of snow at the highest elevations of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of snow fell at the higher elevations.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWEST SHASTA COUNTY TO NORTHERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved through interior Northern California the weekend prior to Thanksgiving.  The storm was cold enough to result in snow falling in the foothill regions of the Coastal, Northern, and Sierra Nevada Mountain ranges while also wet enough to produce more than six feet of snow at the highest elevations of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of snow fell at the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued unsettled pattern remained over the CWA in association with a very active upper level jet and El Nino pattern. The California portion of the Medford CWA was  on the northern fringe of the area of heavy precipitation.  Significant snowfall accumulations were reported for northern California as upslope flow prevailed across the area.  This snow was very wet...about a 6 to 1 snow ration...and this caused many broken trees and branches to fall into power lines...resulting in widespread power outages.   Overall the 1/19-21/10 snowfall in Mt. Shasta City was 39 inches...the greatest 3 day total since 1970.   Widespread power outages began on 1/19/10 (Tuesday). Nearly 9000 Pacific Power customers were without power at the peak of the storm.  By 1/23/10 (Saturday), 1260 customers were still without power.  Virtually all customers had power back by 1/25/10 (Monday).  Siskiyou county, along with other counties in the state, was declared a disaster area due to this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter N Tennant reported 26 inches of snow in 24 hours between 19/1000 and 20/1000 PST.  Based on this and numerous reports of heavy snow in adjacent zone CAZ082, it is assumed that Heavy Snow Warning criteria was met in this zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure subsequently built along the California coast beginning November 14th, bringing a warming trend that saw high temperatures as much as 10 degrees above normal. In addition to the warming trend, the northerly flow around the periphery of the ridge brought gusty winds to the mountains and deserts. The warming trend lasted through the 18th, then the ridge gave way to a deep upper-level trough. This storm system brought a push of unseasonably cold air to the central California interior. Fresno had a high temperature of 74 on November 18th; the next day,|the high was only 60. Bakersfield had a 20-degree change in high temperatures, falling from a high of 79 on November 18th to a high of only 59 degrees on the 19th.|The storm brought the heaviest rainfall for the month to interior central California. The 5-day storm total rainfall for Fresno was 1.23 inch, which accounted for 68.3 percent of the total of 1.80 inch for the month. This 5-day total also was 111.8 % of the normal rainfall for the entire month (1.10 inch). For the same period, Bakersfield recorded 0.54 inch of rain, 64.3 percent of its monthly total of 0.84 inch. The 5-day rainfall nearly matched the normal for November at Bakersfield of 0.59 inch.||In the Southern Sierra Nevada, a total of up to 3 feet of snow fell in some locations above 6000 feet, while in the Kern County Mountains, 4 inches of new snow fell down to 5000 feet. The snow level plunged into the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills, with 7 inches of snow falling at Ponderosa Basin.||Very strong winds occurred in the Kern County Deserts on the afternoon of the 23rd. The highest winds were measured at Blue Max near Walker Pass and Bear Peak in the Southern Sierra. Gusts in those areas topped out at 80-90 mph with a peak gust of 93 mph at 632 pm at Blue Max.

The heaviest rainfall and strongest winds of the week hit the deserts on Thursday...with brief torrential rains that left streets flooded and water in the streams and rivers. At Imperial over two inches of rain fell between the 18th and the 22nd, with 1.10 inches on the 21st. Winds resulted in damage to many areas...and record low barometric pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Motorists reported a tornado crossing Interstate 10 near Intake Blvd, where two semi trucks were blown over. Numerous power poles down, and considerable damage to homes and other structures in and around Blythe. Roofs were blown off some homes. The rain-wrapped tornado had peak wind gusts estimated at 80 mph as it moved toward the northeast. Nearby Blythe airport recorded a peak gust of 55 mph, with thunderstorms in the area. To put this storm in perspective, record low pressure was observed at Blythe,  29.02 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet Pacific low pressure system led to heavy rainfall across Joshua Tree National Park during the morning hours on December 22nd. Radar indicated that between one and three inches of rain fell in the park with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall associated with a wet Pacific storm system began to fall across Joshua Tree National Park during the early morning hours on December 22nd. The heavy rain continued through the morning hours; doppler radar indicated that between one and three inches of rain fell across the park with this storm system. As of 433 am PST, one inch of rain had fallen at the Lost Horse RAWS station. Park rangers indicated that there was some flooding both inside and outside of the park, although flash flooding was not reported.

At Imperial over two inches of rain fell between the 18th and the 22nd, with 1.10 inches on the 21st. Winds resulted in damage to many areas...and record low barometric pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain was reported during a 3 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved along the northern California coast bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the San Francisco Bay Area and central coast. Localized flooding, damaging wind, downed trees and power lines, and minor debris flows all occurred during this last week of the year. More than 77,000 households and businesses lost power during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind blew down a sixty-foot Pine tree which snagged a power line before crashing down onto a pickup truck on Midway Boulevard in Novato. The incident cut power to nearly 3,000 households. Other trees fell, with local power outages reported in San Anselmo, Fairfax, Corte Madera, San Rafael, Kentfield and Bolinas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved along the northern California coast bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the San Francisco Bay Area and central coast. Localized flooding, damaging wind, downed trees and power lines, and minor debris flows all occurred during this last week of the year. More than 77,000 households and businesses lost power during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind knocked down a power pole and transformer onto an Oakley house on Arminio Lane around 9:50 a.m. PST. Elsewhere in Oakley, a six-foot diameter eucalyptus tree was uprooted by the wind and fell onto the empty cab of a big-rig truck. As it was going down it clipped the side of a house in the 2400 block of Delta Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved along the northern California coast bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the San Francisco Bay Area and central coast. Localized flooding, damaging wind, downed trees and power lines, and minor debris flows all occurred during this last week of the year. More than 77,000 households and businesses lost power during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees were knocked down in Sonoma County as strong wind combined with saturated soil. In particular, a man was injured and his vehicle severely damaged when a large tree and power lines fell onto his car shortly after 7:00 a.m. PST.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved along the northern California coast bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the San Francisco Bay Area and central coast. Localized flooding, damaging wind, downed trees and power lines, and minor debris flows all occurred during this last week of the year. More than 77,000 households and businesses lost power during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind knocked over a large Monterey pine in the area of Josselyn Canyon Road and Wagon Wheel Drive. The tree fell around 6:00 p.m. PST and damaged a power pole. About 20 minutes later, another pine tree fell nearby on Twin Oaks Drive, landing on an unoccupied sport utility vehicle. Both pine trees were about 100 feet tall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain fell onto saturated soil causing a minor debris flow to occur along Old San Jose Road. The southbound lane was blocked by mud and debris near the Subud Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rain fell onto saturated soil causing a minor debris flow to occur along Soquel San Jose Road. The northbound lane was blocked by mud and debris near Miller Cut-Off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a minor slide in San Anselmo that resulted in a grove of Eucalyptus trees becoming unstable behind a business on Red Hill Avenue. At least one tree fell over and damaged the business's building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind combined with heavy rain to force a substantial oak tree to fall at the Hotel Yountville in Yountville Sunday afternoon. The Oak tree was to have been a major focus of the landscaping plan. The tree fell onto a new rock wall and damaged a pump house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were blown down across Santa Cruz County, some causing power outages. In particular, a large tree was blown onto Highway 9 in Boulder Creek, which slowed vehicle traffic until crews removed it. Also, power lines were blown down around 9:45 a.m. PST in Woodside causing the closure of State Route 84 at Grandview Road. At approximately 12:42 p.m. PST State Route 35 was closed 2.2 miles north of the Junction of State Route 9 due to downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of low pressure systems, rotating around an upper level low pressure system centered off the coast of the Pacific Northwest, moved through the central California interior, during the night of the 16th into the 17th, bringing copious amounts of moisture to the area. By the evening of the 17th, many locations in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley received from a half inch to over one inch of rain. Locations in the southern Sierra Nevada foothills received up to 3 inches of rain over a 24 hour period. During the evening of the 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th, local roadway flooding, mudslides, and rockslides were reported in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley from Bakersfield northward, in the|mountain areas of Kern County, and in the southern Sierra Nevada foothills.|There was a break in the weather during most of the day of December 18th, but the next storm arrived during the late afternoon and continued overnight and into the next day. By the time midnight arrived, Bakersfield had recorded a record 1.37 inch of rain. Even more rain fell the next day at Meadows Field, shattering rainfall records. There were numerous reports of flooding in the southern Sierra Nevada foothills and on the central and southern San Joaquin Valley floor, as runoff from the heavy rains overwhelmed drainage systems.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough which had been located over the Gulf of Alaska finally progressed toward California bringing brief heavy rain to the San Francisco Bay Area. The brief heavy rain combined with the saturated soil from rains of the past week to cause localized flooding and minor debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain over the previous twelve hours caused the saturated soil along State Highway 1 at Stinson Beach to form a minor landslide. The highway was closed from 2:12 p.m. to 5:11 p.m. PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind caused a four to five foot diameter Douglas fir tree to fall into State Route 9 just south of Glengarry Road in the Santa Cruz area. The road was closed from 4:46 p.m. to 6:32 p.m. PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving storm spread brief heavy rain and strong wind across the San Francisco's North Bay Area blowing trees down resulting in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with strong wind to cause numerous trees to fall down. On Porter Creek Road at about 5 a.m. PST a tree blew onto a passing car and knocked power lines down causing the road to be closed. On Graton Road at Upp Road another tree was blown onto power lines blocking the road in both directions. Additional trees were reported down on Martinelli Road about 7:15 a.m. and on Harrison Grade Road around 11:25 a.m.  In all, about 2,200 customers lost power in the North Bay from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind caused a large tree to tumble onto State Route 35 near John Daly Road in Daly City. The road was closed at 1:07 a.m. PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were blown down across Santa Cruz County, some causing power outages. In particular, a large Acacia tree was blown over about 10:30 a.m. PST causing a section of Freedom Boulevard between Hames and Pleasant Valley roads to shut down. Around 400 homes and businesses lost power early Sunday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold low pressure dropped south along the Pacific Northwest coast and into the northern Sierra between the 18th and the 21st followed by another low on the 23rd. This brought gusty winds and periods of heavy snow to northeast California and the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2 main periods of snow with this slow moving storm, with one period from late evening on the 19th to the morning of the 20th and the second period about 24 hours later. Much more snow fell west of highway 395 than along and east, with storm totals of 2.5 to 3.5 feet near the Sierra crest and only 6 to 12 inches along and east of highway 395.  In addition to snowfall, winds were gusting 35 to 50 mph on the 19th with a few gusts over 60 mph from 1400PST to 1700PST for Walker RAWS. Finally, a man was killed near Mammoth Lakes when his vehicle went off a road in low visibility conditions due to heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold low pressure remained off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days bringing very heavy snow to the Sierra and periods of strong, damaging winds. The very heavy snowfall increased the high Sierra snowpack from 150 to 160 percent of normal to over 220 percent of normal in just four days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals reached a widespread 5 to 8 feet for the high Sierra, with almost 10 feet for the Mammoth ski area. For elevations below 7500 feet, 1 to 2 feet was reported with some rain at times below 7000 feet. Heavy snow caused the closure of highway 395 on the 19th. As far as winds, widespread wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph were widespread on the 19th, with winds up to 64 mph at the Walker RAWS. For the higher elevations, a 164 mph gust was noted at the  Mammoth ski area chair 1. Strong winds caused some downed trees and power lines in Mammoth Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two significant systems within 3 days moved through the region on the 18th and brought moderate amounts of snowfall, gusty winds, and an extended closure of highway 395 between Bridgeport and Mammoth Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread snowfall of 10 to 18 inches was reported per SNOTEL and CO-OP observations. The exceptions were near South Lake Tahoe where slightly warmer temperatures initially limited snowfall with around 8 inches at the Fallen Leaf SNOTEL.||In addition to snowfall, wind gusts to 90 mph were reported over the Sierra Crest while gusty winds of 30-45 mph were recorded from the Truckee AWOS, Alpine Meadows Ski Area, and South Lake Tahoe ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air behind a cold front brought snow levels down to as low as 2000 feet with significant snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Somes Bar RAWS measured 0.43 inches of precipitation between 09/1716 PST and 10/1116 PST with temperatures 32F to 38F in the same period. Using a wet snow ratio of 6:1 produces 7 inches, which meets Heavy Snow Warning criteria (7+ inches in 12 hours). It should be noted that the precipitation recording was delayed until temperatures increased above freezing later in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of two significant systems within 3 days moved through the region on the 18th and brought moderate amounts of snowfall, gusty winds, and an extended closure of highway 395 between Bridgeport and Mammoth Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event featured a very intense period of snowfall for the afternoon and evening of the 18th. For example, 12 and 14 inches of snowfall in 7 hours was recorded for the Lobdell Lake and Sonora Pass SNOTELs, respectively. Other snowfall totals for the entire event include: 25 at the Mammoth Ski Area, 13 inches at the Lee Vining CO-OP, 9 inches at Bodie State Park, and 5 inches in Bridgeport.||In addition to heavy snowfall, winds gusted over 120 mph atop Mammoth Mountain and between 35-45 mph at the Bridgeport RAWS and Mammoth AWOS (with one gust to 68 mph for Bridgeport). Finally, highway 395 was closed from 1730PST on the 18th until late on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold low pressure remained off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days bringing very heavy snow to the Sierra and periods of strong, damaging winds. The very heavy snowfall increased the high Sierra snowpack from 150 to 160 percent of normal to over 220 percent of normal in just four days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas SW of Susanville received 10 to 20 inches below 7000 feet, with an estimated 2 to 4 feet (based on precip gauges and SNOTELS) above 7000 feet. Elsewhere, much less fell with only up to 6 inches reported. In addition to snow, gusty winds of 30 to 45 mph were widespread on the 19th. A local wind gust to 83 mph was reported just west of Loyalton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold low pressure remained off the Pacific Northwest coast for several days bringing very heavy snow to the Sierra and periods of strong, damaging winds. The very heavy snowfall increased the high Sierra snowpack from 150 to 160 percent of normal to over 220 percent of normal in just four days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski resorts accumulated an average of 5 to 8 feet of snow. Twenty to 30 inches of snow fell north and west of Lake Tahoe below 7000 feet. However, valley areas south and east of Lake Tahoe received as little as 12 inches with periods of rain. Heavy wet snow caused a rock slide on highway 50 which closed the road on the 19th. In addition to snow, numerous reports of downed trees and associated damage were noted on the 19th. Falling trees were responsible for one destroyed house in South Lake Tahoe, with winds gusting to 63 mph and 68 mph in Truckee and South Lake Tahoe, respectively, and up to 155 mph over the crest. Finally, a tree fell on a ski lift cable at the Sierra-at-Tahoe resort and caused skiers to be ejected and injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of powerful winter storms wreaked havoc on Southern California on January 19th. A strong southerly 925 mb jet ahead of the cold front contributed to moderate low-level wind shear. A line of thunderstorms with embedded rotation moved through Orange County late in the morning, bringing waterspouts, at least one tornado, and hurricane force winds. Strong thunderstorm winds also impacted San Diego County, contributing to structural damage and one fatality. Large, steep surf was also observed with this storm in both Orange and San Diego counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds associated with the passage of the cold front combined with rain soaked soils caused a tree to collapse in Scripps Ranch. The tree crashed through a roof and into a bedroom of a house, causing major damage. No one was injured. A large tree branch fell in Ramona, damaging part of a chain link fence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed off the coast of Sunset Beach in Orange County and moved Northeast. The tornado crossed Pacific Coast Highway just south of Anderson Street at 1257 PST and continued to move Northeast. A parked Ford Explorer was flipped on its side, and two 35 foot catamarans in the Huntington Harbour were lifted out of the water. One was lifted 50 feet in the air before landing on another vessel and a dock piling. The second was flipped over and landed 30 feet from its original position. There were multiple reports of roof damage, and a window in a residential building was blown in. A wind gust of 92 MPH was measured by a mesonet at the Huntington Beach Pier.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of thunderstorms moved through San Diego county around 1500 PST. In Santee, winds tore off the roofs of three mobile homes, with some parts of the roof flying as much as 75 feet away. In Flinn Springs, a 10 foot diameter tree fell onto a mobile home, killing a woman. The San Miguel RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 59 MPH during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fourth, and strongest, in a series of winter storms pounded the region on the 21st and 22nd, bringing another round of gusty winds, heavy rain, thunderstorms, several feet of snow in the mountains, and flooding throughout the CWA. Many roads in the region were closed due to flooding and mud on the roadway, as well as fallen trees. In Horsethief Canyon Ranch in Temescal Valley dozens of large trees were uprooted due to the heavy rain and winds, though no other damage was reported. The storm was marked by record-breaking low pressure. Lindbergh Field recorded a sea level pressure of 987.1 mb, shattering the old record of 994.6 mb set on March 3 1983.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Bernardino County Works reported a debris flow on Pendleton Road in Yucaipa. The hills just above the road had been burned in the 2009 Pendleton Fire. The street was closed off due to flooding, but no other damage was reported. Mud was as deep as 4 feet in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of convection moved through the San Diego County coastal areas between 1800 and 1900 PST. A clogged storm drain caused flooding at the intersection of Via de la Valle and Jimmy Durante Boulevard in Del Mar. Eight vehicles became trapped in the rapidly rising water, and eighteen people, including several children, had to be rescued by swift-water rescue teams and local lifeguards. There were no injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A torrey pine tree fell on a home on Rimini Road in Del Mar. The time of the incident is unknown. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 51 MPH were measured by the Ramona Airport ASOS. The strong gusty winds began around 0730 PST on the 21st and continued through about 2300 PST on the 22nd. During that time, an oak tree fell on a car driving on Mossey Grade Road in Ramona, trapping two teenagers. Neither were injured. Another large tree was uprooted and fell on a home on 11th Street in Ramona. Numerous other trees fell down around the city. On family on Highland Valley Road lost 17 avocado trees and a pole that powers their well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm moved across northern and central California producing heavy rain and strong wind. Numerous power outages, trees blown down, localized flooding and minor debris flows occurred during this storm. Throughout the weekend about 3,200 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Valley Oak trees, next to each other, were blown down in Cloverdale. The two trees were estimated to be over 100 years old and were between 70 and 80 feet tall. There were no injuries; however, the Snack Shack and an adjacent building sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 inch diameter eucalyptus tree fell on the 400 block of Jasmine Street in Laguna Beach. The tree damaged a fence and gate opener between two houses.  At Doheny State Beach, a eucalyptus tree fell on a camper shell. No injuries were reported with either event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the San Bernardino County mountains around 1200 PST. Spotters in Wrightwood, Forest Falls, and Blue Jay reported blizzard conditions throughout the day, with visibility near zero at times. Wind gusts in the area were around 40 MPH. By 1700 PST, Wrightwood had received twelve inches of snow. The snow finally ended around 2130 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Riverside County mountains around 1230 PST. A trained spotter in Pine Cove reported that approximately 8 inches fell by 1700 PST, with an additional 1.5 inches falling through about 2330 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were measured by the Burns Canyon RAWS between 1151 and 1451 PST. Between 1251 and 1351 PST, a peak gust of 85 MPH was measured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Diego River crested just below the 11.3 foot flood stage on the afternoon of the 21st. There were reports of flooding along Ward Road and in the Qualcomm Stadium parking lot in Mission Valley. No damage was reported.

A weather spotter estimated wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH in San Diego Country Estates around 1230 PST. Two large trees were blown down and blocking the road near San Diego Country Estates.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Mecca, eight homes had to be evacuated due to flooding. Some residents claimed that flooding has been a problem for years. An auto wrecking business in Indio was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow occurred across northern California with heavy snow accumulations as a result of the enhanced precipitation processes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Somes Bar RAWS (elevation 920 feet) measured 1.45 inches of precipitation in a 19 hour period between 12/0216 PST and 12/2116 PST. Using a wet snow ratio of 7:1, produces 10 inches of snow with surface temperatures observed between 37F to 47F. It is assumed that measuring of precipitation was delayed until snow melt and that Heavy Snow Warning criteria was met for CAZ080 earlier in the day. In addition, the Scott Mt weather station (elevation 5900 feet) measured 1.13 inches of precipitation in 7 hours with surface temperatures 26F to 33F between 12/1000 PST and 12/1700 PST, which comes out to 8 inches in the 7 hours, meeting Heavy Snow Warning criteria. Lastly, the Happy Camp portable weather station (1500 feet) measured 1.22 inches of precipitation between 12/0445 PST and 12/2145 PST with surface temperatures between 32F and 43F (delayed measurement due to snowfall not melting into gage until temperatures rose above freezing).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Bernardino County Public Works reported debris and mud in several homes in Yucaipa beginning around 1236 PST and continuing through about 1400 PST. The flash flooding may have knocked over power lines along Wildwood Canyon Road near 2nd Street. The flash flood was a result of heavy rain in the region. Large amounts of water and mud flowed out of the hills to the east of Yucaipa and through the Wildwood Canyon Wash. It is likely that the majority of the flooding was due to the wash overflowing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began in the Riverside County mountains shortly after midnight on the 21st. Based on rainfall totals at lower elevations, snowfall in the San Bernardino County mountains, and spotter reports, approximately 12 inches of snow fell by 1530 PST. Snowfall finally ended by about 2000 PST on the 22nd. By the time snowfall ended, the Palm Springs Aerial Tramway reported a weekly total of 5 feet of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow occurred across northern California with heavy snow accumulations as a result of the enhanced precipitation processes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Caltrans Snowmans Summit station (elevation 4478 feet) measured 1.37 inches of precipitation in a 19 hour period between 11/2340 PST and 12/1810 PST. Using a wet snow ratio of 7:1, produces 10 inches of snow with surface temperatures observed between 28F to 31F, which meets Heavy Snow Warning criteria.  This station is in zone CAZ082, but it is representative of portions of CAZ083.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a 20 foot by 20 foot section of roof to collapse at an industrial building on Valley View St in La Palma around 1645 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow occurred across northern California with heavy snow accumulations as a result of the enhanced precipitation processes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mt. Shasta ASOS (elevation 3533 feet) measured 0.96 inches of precipitation in a 19 hour period between 11/2256 PST and 12/1756 PST. Using a wet snow ratio of 7:1, produces 7 inches of snow with surface temperatures observed between 29F to 36F. It is assumed that measuring of precipitation was delayed until snow melt and that Heavy Snow Warning criteria was met for CAZ082 earlier in the day. The Mount Shasta City RAWS recorded 1.13 inches of precipitation in 19 hours between 12/2252 and 13/1752 PST with a temperature ranging from 29F to 35F.  At higher elevations, this likely translated to at least 8 inches of snow.  The Caltrans Snowmans Summit station (elevation 4478 feet) measured 1.37 inches of precipitation in a 19 hour period between 11/2340 PST and 12/1810 PST. Using a wet snow ratio of 7:1, produces 10 inches of snow with surface temperatures observed between 28F to 31F, which meets Heavy Snow Warning criteria.

The storm was marked by record-breaking low pressure. Lindbergh Field recorded a sea level pressure of 987.1 mb, shattering the old record of 994.6 mb set on March 3 1983.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began in the San Bernardino County Mountains around 0230 PST on January 21st and continued through about 2200 PST on the 22nd. Several spotters reported 24 hour snow accumulations of 24 to over 32 inches. Some areas received as much as 3 to 4 feet of snow within 36 hours. By the end of the storm, Snow Valley Ski Resort had a total of 80 to 92 inches of new snow for the week, Bear Mountain Ski Resort had 60 to 66 inches, and a spotter in Big Bear City reported 57 inches. Several mobile homes were damaged due to the heavy snow. A 30 foot tall cedar tree fell under the weight of the snow, damaging a fence and some outdoor tables at Broadway Cafe in Big Bear City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Diego County Flood Control relayed a report from the public of numerous locations of flooded roadways with mud, rocks, and debris along Highway 76 through the La Jolla Reservation. A newspaper reported two feet of mud in the garage of a home in Rincon. This occurred after heavy rain moved over the 2007 Poomacha Fire burn area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third storm of the week moved into Southern California on the morning of January 20th, bringing yet another round of widespread heavy rain and mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began around 1052 PST and continued through 1254 PST. At 1952 PST, the Victorville ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 62 MPH. A CWOP station in Hesperia reported a peak gust of 61 MPH at 1254 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fourth, and strongest, in a series of winter storms pounded the region on the 21st and 22nd, bringing another round of gusty winds, heavy rain, thunderstorms, several feet of snow in the mountains, and flooding throughout the CWA. Many roads in the region were closed due to flooding and mud on the roadway, as well as fallen trees. In Horsethief Canyon Ranch in Temescal Valley dozens of large trees were uprooted due to the heavy rain and winds, though no other damage was reported. The storm was marked by record-breaking low pressure. Lindbergh Field recorded a sea level pressure of 987.1 mb, shattering the old record of 994.6 mb set on March 3 1983.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were measured by several RAWS in the Riverside County Mountains between 1230 PST and 2330 PST. Strong Southwest winds ahead of a cold front peaked around 1300 PST. At that time, the Angelus Hill RAWS measured a peak gust of 67 MPH, while the Ranger Peak and Vista Grande RAWS both measured peak gusts of 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of powerful winter storms affected Central and Southern California between the 18th and 22nd of January. As this series of storms moved across the area, they brought heavy rain, flash flooding, gusty winds, heavy snow and even severe weather to the area. By the 22nd, rainfall totals for this series of storms ranged from 4-8 inches over coastal areas to 8-16 inches in the foothills and mountains. Due to some very intense periods of rainfall, flash flooding and mud and debris flows occurred across the area. In the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties, 1 to 3 feet of new snowfall was reported. Strong southerly winds were common as each storm moved across the area with wind gusts as high as 71 MPH reported in some spots. Along with the rain and snow, some severe weather occurred across the area with reports of waterspouts, straight-line winds and even a weak tornado in the city of Ventura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms moved over the Jesusita Burn area, producing intense rainfall rates and some damage associated with mud and debris flows. Automated rain gages in the area indicated hourly rainfall rates between 0.75 and 1.00 inches which exceeding established USGS thresholds. Per reports from emergency managers in Santa Barbara county, the heavy rainfall produced some mud and debris flows which broke sections of some debris basins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system driving into the Pacific Northwest dragged a cold front through northeast California on the 14th and 15th. This brought gusty winds along with a moderate Sierra snowfall and a light snowfall for some lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain until around 1400PST cut down snowfall totals to 6 to 8 inches below 7000 ft, with 8 to 12 inches west of Lake Tahoe. Near the crest, 10 to 15 inches of snow fell. In addition to snow, winds gusted to 30 to 45 mph through the afternoon of the 14th, with around 100 mph over the crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By January 13th, the medium-range forecast models were becoming consistent in predicting a major precipitation event that would affect interior central California beginning the 17th and continuing for several days. A series of Special Weather Statements were issued to prepare emergency officials and the public on the upcoming change in the weather, followed by Winter Storm Watches for the Southern Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains, beginning January 16th and upgraded to Warnings the next day.||Global composite satellite loops on January 17th showed a series of at least 5 distinct storms stretching roughly along the 40-degree latitude line from the California coast westward across the Pacific and into northeastern China. This line of storms had similarities to the storms of January-February 1998 that brought flooding to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, but there were some significant differences.||While both events occurred during El Ninos, the 1998 event brought warm, subtropical moisture that eroded the mountain snowpack and appreciably added to the runoff. While the event of January 2010 was similarly moisture-laden, surges of cold air kept snow levels low, and snow even fell on the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills on January 21st-22nd. In addition, the spacing between the storms in the recent event allowed for several-hour breaks between the first few storms, enabling the ground to absorb some of the moisture before the runoff from the next storm hit. This is not to say that was not some flooding in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, but most was either due to clogged storm drains or occurred in normally flood-prone areas.||The first storm moved rapidly through central California during the afternoon and evening of January 17th. Despite its fast movement, the storm dropped around a half inch of rain over much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley.||A stronger storm followed the next day, bringing severe weather to the central California interior. Strong winds over the Tehachapi Mountains ahead of the storm caused the first of two storm-related fatalities during the week-long event. At about 9:30 AM, winds toppled at tree near Pine Mountain Club, west of Frazier Park. The tree fell onto a house, killing the occupant. Later that day, a brief EF0 tornado���the first of the year for interior central California���was reported on the west side of Fresno at 3:23 PM during the afternoon of the Martin Luther King, Jr. holiday on the 18th. There was no damage reported with this tornado. The following write-up is from the tabular January climate summary for Bakersfield and Fresno.||The January 18th Fresno tornado: Thunderstorms over the central San Joaquin Valley during the mid afternoon of January 18th spawned a brief tornado 5 miles southwest of the city of Fresno. At 1523 pst, a weather spotter reported a tornado north of Highway 180. Video of the tornado showed a rain-wrapped funnel extending from a rotating supercell, and National Weather Service doppler radar indicated a velocity couplet near the time and location of the report. The tornado was rated EF0 on the enhanced fujita scale. This tornado was the first tornado in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley since the Atwater tornado of February 9th, 2009. The last tornado in Fresno county was on May 9th, 2005, near the city of Fowler.||The third storm reached interior central California on the 19th, and an upper-level disturbance rotating around the low moved into southern California, bringing snow to the Tehachapi Mountains. Snow levels with this storm were lower than expected-down to around 4000 feet. Runoff resulted in some road flooding, and creeks in rural areas ran high. The other storm-related fatality occurred that evening, when a man drove around barriers in an attempt to cross a flooded road near the Merced County-Stanislaus County line. The road had been flooded by the Orestimba Creek, and the driver was swept away by the fast current.||Storm number 4 reached central California around midday of January 20th, and pushed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours. This storm dropped snow on the Grapevine and triggered isolated early evening thunderstorms. Also, strong winds occurred in the central valley. Bakersfield reported a wind gust around noon of 46 mph; Merced had a 48 mph sustained wind with a 61 mph wind gust around 2:00 PM that afternoon. There were numerous reports of wind damage, including downed trees and broken roof shingles, throughout the valley that week, especially on the 20th.||The last storm of the series arrived on the 21st, bringing very cold air to the region. Snow levels dropped to around 2200 feet, with snow falling in the towns of Mariposa and Oakhurst. Both the Grapevine and Tehachapi Pass were closed for several hours by the snow, hindering travel between the San Joaquin Valley and southern California. This was a very deep low pressure system, and all-time low pressure records were set in both Bakersfield and Fresno; both reported 28.94 inches of mercury. This storm also brought very strong winds to the Kern County Mountains where 10 inch diameter tree branches were downed in the Grapevine area due to winds approaching 70 mph in gusts there.||The cold pool of air moved over the area on the afternoon of January 22nd, triggering strong convective showers, one of which blanketed parts of the city of Clovis with about 2-3 inches deep of pea-size hail on the ground; a funnel cloud was reported near Clovis Ave and State Route 168 in Clovis.||By the time the last storm moved east of the region, the total rainfall amounts in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley mostly were between 1.5 and 2.5 inches, with a few locations around 3 inches. Snowfall amounts in the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains were measured in feet, with the heaviest snowfalls reaching around 10 feet of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported flooding of Herring Rd exit on Hwy 99 and flooding of Hwy 33 From Hwy 46 to Lerdo Hwy.

The following write-up is from the tabular January climate summary for Bakersfield and Fresno.||The January 18th Fresno tornado: Thunderstorms over the central San Joaquin Valley during the mid afternoon of January 18th spawned a brief tornado 5 miles southwest of the city of Fresno. At 1523 pst, a weather spotter reported a tornado north of Highway 180. Video of the tornado showed a rain-wrapped funnel extending from a rotating supercell, and National Weather Service doppler radar indicated a velocity couplet near the time and location of the report. The tornado was rated EF0 on the enhanced fujita scale. This tornado was the first tornado in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley since the Atwater tornado of February 9th, 2009. Snow levels with this storm were lower than expected-down to around 4000 feet. Runoff resulted in some road flooding, and creeks in rural areas ran high. The other storm-related fatality occurred that evening, when a man drove around barriers in an attempt to cross a flooded road near the Merced County-Stanislaus County line. The road had been flooded by the Orestimba Creek, and the driver was swept away by the fast current.||Storm number 4 reached central California around midday of January 20th, and pushed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours. This storm dropped snow on the Grapevine and triggered isolated early evening thunderstorms. Also, strong winds occurred in the central valley. Bakersfield reported a wind gust around noon of 46 mph; Merced had a 48 mph sustained wind with a 61 mph wind gust around 2:00 PM that afternoon. There were numerous reports of wind damage, including downed trees and broken roof shingles, throughout the valley that week, especially on the 20th.||The last storm of the series arrived on the 21st, bringing very cold air to the region. Snow levels dropped to around 2200 feet, with snow falling in the towns of Mariposa and Oakhurst. Both the Grapevine and Tehachapi Pass were closed for several hours by the snow, hindering travel between the San Joaquin Valley and southern California. This was a very deep low pressure system, and all-time low pressure records were set in both Bakersfield and Fresno; both reported 28.94 inches of mercury. Snowfall amounts in the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains were measured in feet, with the heaviest snowfalls reaching around 10 feet of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local highway officials reported flooding on Hwy 198 at Hwy 269.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved along the northern California coast bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the San Francisco Bay Area and central coast. Localized flooding, damaging wind, downed trees and power lines, and minor debris flows all occurred during this last week of the year. More than 77,000 households and businesses lost power during this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind and saturated soil caused trees to be uprooted in portions of the northern Big Sur coast and near Monterey. In Carmel Highlands, a tree was blown onto a house causing some damage. In the Monterey area wind removed tree branches which severed power lines resulting in numerous power outages across the peninsula. In all, more than 10,600 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By January 13th, the medium-range forecast models were becoming consistent in predicting a major precipitation event that would affect interior central California beginning the 17th and continuing for several days. In addition, the spacing between the storms in the recent event allowed for several-hour breaks between the first few storms, enabling the ground to absorb some of the moisture before the runoff from the next storm hit. Localized flooding, damaging wind, downed trees and power lines, and minor debris flows all occurred during this last week of the year.
