An incoming cold front brought heavy precipitation to portions of northern California, but due to high snow levels...around 4500 feet...no reports of high snow were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An incoming cold front brought heavy precipitation to portions of northern California, but due to high snow levels...around 4500 feet...no reports of high snow were received.  However, heavy rain reports were recorded in western Siskiyou county, including 1.24 inches in 24 hours at Happy Camp.  Based on these reports, it is estimated that heavy snow occurred at higher elevations in zone CAZ080.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved northward throughout the Imperial Valley with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds uprooted about 26 date palms, and damaged buildings in Bard and Winterhaven. Approximately half of the roof of a local community center in Bard collapsed, in addition to the roof of a business called the Old Bard Store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved northward throughout the Imperial Valley with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to ping pong ball size was reported by the public in El Centro.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Maywood Fire began at approximately 1038 PST on July 17th near the intersection of Santa Rosa Mine Road and Christmas Tree Lane in Good Hope, near Perris, California. The cause of the fire is yet unknown, but was contained around 1545 PST on the same day. Two firefighters were evaluated by paramedics for heat-related injuries.

The Cactus Fire began at approximately 1400 PST on July 15th near the intersection of Red Mountain road and Owl Creek, near the city of Sage, California. The fire, along with a few other small fires in the Sage-Anza-Pinyon area, was started by lightning and burned a total of 706 acres before being contained at 0600 PST on the 18th. Mandatory evacuations of the residents of Cactus Valley and R3, Limb Road and Old Ranch Road were ordered around 1930 PST on the 15th. Residents were not allowed back into the area until the night of the 18th. No damage or injuries were reported with this fire.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Skinner Fire began at approximately 1442 PST on July 15th near the intersection of Crown Valley Road and De Portola Road near the city of Temecula, California. The fire was started by lightning and grew quickly to 200 acres within a few hours. Residents in the area were evacuated and allowed to return to their homes around 2100 PST on the 15th. The fire grew to 503 acres before being contained around 0600 PST on the 18th. No damage or injuries were reported with this fire.

Temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley warmed into the 90s by June 5th and 6th. Although Coalinga had a high of 99 on the 5th, no reporting station hit triple digits. In addition to keeping a high pressure area in check���and temperatures under 100���upper level low pressure areas passing just to the north did bring several periods of very windy conditions to the region. The strongest winds were over the Kern County Mountains and deserts, where gusts of 50-55 mph were a nightly occurrence, lasting several hours at a time, in the area immediately below the southeast-facing passes and canyons of the Tehachapi Mountains. The winds were most prevalent from the night of June 3rd through the morning of the 10th, with the winds reaching a peak on the night of June 8th at nearly 80 mph at Mojave, just below Tehachapi Pass, where the busy highways 14 and 58 intersect.||A high pressure ridge also brought warming to the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada during the first half of June, resulting in rapid erosion of the snowpack. As the snow melted, runoff filled the rivers and streams below. The Merced River rose to its flood stage at Pohono Bridge during the night of June 5th-6th, receded during the day, and rose above flood stage again that night. Elsewhere in the mountains and foothills, the cold, fast-moving water was a risk to boaters, swimmers and rafters. The National Weather Service and local media partners issued statements cautioning any visitors to the mountains on the dangers of hypothermia and other water hazards. Every year sees several people killed due to the very cold, fast flowing deep waters of area rivers and streams.  This year was no different.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Pacific Ocean caused a six to eight foot swell to travel into the coastal waters of California. Breaking waves occurred near the shore causing hazardous surf in some locations, including the waters north of Point Reyes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24-foot motorized fishing boat capsized in the six to eight foot surf about one mile offshore of Ten Mile Beach, also known as The Great Beach, north of Point Reyes. The four fishermen aboard lost their lives as they were thrown into the frigid water.  None of them were wearing life vests. Also, the boat was totally destroyed with parts of the boat washing up along more than one mile of shoreline.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged about 5 degrees above normal during the first week of June and accelerated snowmelt over the higher elevations of the Sierra which led to an increase in water levels on all rivers above the major reservoirs.  As a result, rising waters along the upper Merced River produced minor flooding in Yosemite National Park during the first weekend of the month until about mid morning on June 8th, at which time the river finally began to recede as cooler air invaded the region.  The|Merced River at Pohono Bridge first rose to flood stage during the early morning hours of  the 6th with diurnal fluctuations just above flood stage during the overnight hours of the 6th and 7th.  Park officials reported only minor impacts including evacuating camp sites near the river in Yosemite Valley. No property damage was reported due to the flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific storms moved across interior Northern California bringing heavy snow, strong winds, and dangerous travel conditions to the mountains.  Storm totals in the northern Sierra Nevada mountains measured up to 5 feet above 7000 feet, with amounts of 2 to 3 feet near 5000 feet.||Strong winds, heavy rain, and thunderstorms impacted the Central Valley on the 19th and 20th. An all-time record low barometric pressure was set for Sacramento on the 21st. The lowest sea level pressure at Sacramento Executive Airport was 28.94 inches at 2:46 pm PST. This broke the previous record of 28.95 inches, set on January 27th at the old U.S. Weather Bureau Office in downtown Sacramento.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell at the higher elevations including six inches at Shasta City and two inches in the foothills north of Redding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to another foot of snow fell across the area less than a day following the previous storm.  Winds were not as strong but roadways warmed slightly between the storms and became icy before the latest layer of snow.  This resulted in even more treacherous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms dropped very heavy rains over parts of Joshua Tree National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms resulted in flooded roads near Queen Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited several trees and started a 10 acre fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches of new snow fell across the highest elevations less than a day following the previous storm.  Winds were not as strong but roadways warmed slightly between the storms and became icy before the latest layer of snow... resulting in treacherous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system brought wintry conditions to the higher elevations. This caused travel difficulties over the mountain passes and higher roads. Storm total snow amounts up to 17 inches were reported above 8000 feet in the northern Sierra Nevada mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow fell across the highest elevations with five inches falling at Shasta City.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN MONTEREY BAY AND BIG SUR COAST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Pacific Ocean caused an eight to ten foot swell to travel into the coastal waters of California. At the coast, larger sets of waves, called sneaker waves, can move farther up the beaches than expected and catch visitors by surprise. These types of waves occurred at Monastery Beach, south of Carmel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As many as ten people at Monastery Beach, south of Carmel, were caught by surprise as a sneaker wave washed them from the beach into the ocean. A Good Samaritan decided to help with the rescue and died after pulling at least one person to safety. Another beachgoer succumbed to injuries and died the next day. Five others were taken to local hospitals and then released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell on the northern Sierra Nevada Mountains, the mountains of western Plumas County, and in Lassen Volcanic National Park. Total snow amounts were 12 to 18 inches over the higher mountain elevations.  Locally gusty winds and the resulting blowing snow produced poor visibilities and travel difficulties over mountain passes.||Snow accumulations were generally light to moderate over the Coastal Mountains and the mountains of Shasta County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to eight inches of heavy snow fell at the highest elevations including three inches in Burney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and unstable air mass produced strong thunderstorms across Ventura county. Large hail between one half inch and one inch was reported along with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported one inch hail in association with a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough of low pressure moving east over the region brought widespread rain, mountain snow, thunderstorms, and urban flooding February 5th through 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain and saturated soil caused the ground beneath Highway 74 near Hemet to erode, taking with it a 30 foot by 40 foot section of the road. The highway was closed in both directions for several days for repairs. The incident occurred around 1340 PST in the Cottonwood Fire burn area, however, it was determined that a debris flow was not the cause. No other damage and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff low pressure system moved inland just south of the International Border bringing numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms to the region. Rainfall was widespread and generous in amount, causing numerous reports of urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a trailer located in the 18000 block of Danbury Avenue around 0800 PST on October 19, setting it on fire. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture fueled several rounds of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert, with the greatest coverage of storms on the 26th and 27th.  Several storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris flowed over several roads in the eastern part of Barstow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The stormy pattern from early October continued into the 6th and 7th of the month with more flooding and storms around the interior of Central California.||On October 6th, the showers continued over the Sierra and much of the San Joaquin Valley, including the west side. Flooding was reported on Highway 33 near Taft due to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours, as moisture remained quite abundant and wrapped around an upper level low centered near the coast of central California. By the 7th, the low had ejected eastward toward the northern Rockies, and finally shut off the moisture over the over as an upper-level high pressure ridge began to approach from the Pacific Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reports water over the road at CA Highway 33 and Midway Road in Fellows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of significant storms brought strong winds and heavy rain to the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas. This storm, the strongest of the week, developed over the Pacific Ocean with a strong parent low pressure based in the Gulf of Alaska. Around 159,000 customers lost power across the San Francisco Bay area with nearly 22,000 customers without power in the Monterey Bay area. Numerous power lines and trees were knocked down when strong wind combined with saturated soil. Also, areas of flooding occurred causing mainly problems for vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the San Lorenzo River in Felton to rise over its banks and flood the Felton Grove community. Two hundred residents were called to evacuate before the river rose to 18 feet and overflowed into the streets, including River Road and Sylvan Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of four Pacific storms pounded the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin between January 18th and 21st with heavy rain and snow (which led to river flooding), locally high winds, and isolated severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 95 was closed south of Interstate 40 due to flooding and roadway damage.  Portions of the highway were closed for a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain and flash flooding to Southern California. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 1.50 to 2.50 inches over coastal areas with up to 4.50 inches over the foothills and mountains. With such heavy rainfall, the burn areas of Los Angeles county experienced several episodes of flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported mud and debris flows near the intersection of the Pacific Coast Highway and Topanga Canyon Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff low pressure system moved inland just south of the International Border bringing numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms to the region. Rainfall was widespread and generous in amount, causing numerous reports of urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single family residence received a direct lightning strike to the roof, creating a 3-inch diameter hole. No injuries or fire, but the American Red Cross was called to assist the 5 adults and one infant residents.
